question: What is the effect of providing a mapping instruction between a source catch event and a target catch event during process instance migration?

options.text: The associated event subscription from the source catch event is preserved (migrated) onto the target catch event.
The source subscription is closed and a brand-new subscription is opened on the target catch event.
The mapping is ignored at runtime; catch event subscriptions only change when the instance later reaches the catch event.
An incident is raised if the source and target catch events differ in name or type.

a.text: Mapping a catch event preserves the existing event subscription. Timers keep their elapsed time, and message or signal subscriptions keep their correlation state.

b.text: Closing the subscription and opening a new one is what happens when the source catch event is NOT mapped — the opposite of providing a mapping instruction.

c.text: Mapping instructions take effect at migration time. The subscription state is updated immediately, not lazily when execution next reaches the element.

d.text: Mapping a renamed or differently-defined target is the documented way to migrate a subscription onto a new element; it is not an error.

explanation: Providing a mapping instruction between source and target catch events migrates the associated subscription. State carried by that subscription — such as the elapsed time of a timer or a buffered message correlation — is preserved on the target catch event after migration.
